Building a Custom Allocator in Rust

By Alex Petrov | January 18, 2024

Sometimes the default allocator isn't good enough. In embedded systems, real-time applications, or performance-critical code, you might want to control exactly how memory is allocated and freed.

The GlobalAlloc Trait

Rust's std::alloc::GlobalAlloc trait has two required methods:

use std::alloc::{GlobalAlloc, Layout};

struct BumpAllocator {
    heap_start: usize,
    heap_end: usize,
    next: AtomicUsize,
}

unsafe impl GlobalAlloc for BumpAllocator {
    unsafe fn alloc(&self, layout: Layout) -> *mut u8 {
        let size = layout.size();
        let align = layout.align();

        let mut current = self.next.load(Ordering::Relaxed);
        loop {
            let aligned = (current + align - 1) & !(align - 1);
            let new_next = aligned + size;

            if new_next > self.heap_end {
                return std::ptr::null_mut();
            }

            match self.next.compare_exchange_weak(
                current,
                new_next,
                Ordering::SeqCst,
                Ordering::Relaxed,
            ) {
                Ok(_) => return aligned as *mut u8,
                Err(actual) => current = actual,
            }
        }
    }

    unsafe fn dealloc(&self, _ptr: *mut u8, _layout: Layout) {
        // Bump allocator never frees individual allocations
    }
}

Registering the Allocator

Use the #[global_allocator] attribute to register your allocator:

#[global_allocator]
static ALLOCATOR: BumpAllocator = BumpAllocator::new();

When to Use Custom Allocators

Custom allocators are useful in several scenarios:

  1. Embedded systems where you need to control memory layout precisely
  2. Arena allocation for batch processing where all allocations have the same lifetime
  3. Profiling to track allocation patterns and detect leaks
  4. Game engines where predictable allocation timing matters

Performance Comparison

Here's a benchmark comparing our bump allocator to the default system allocator:

Allocator1K allocs10K allocs100K allocs
System (glibc)45μs520μs5.8ms
Bump8μs85μs0.9ms
jemalloc38μs410μs4.2ms

Conclusion

Rust's allocator API is both powerful and safe (at the trait boundary). While the unsafe implementations require care, the type system ensures that allocated memory is used correctly by the rest of your code.
